content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN nonnegative integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. Determine if there exist nonnegative integers B,CB, CB,C such that the following condition holds:

(A1+B)⊕C<(A2+B)⊕C<…<(AN+B)⊕C(A_1 + B) \oplus C \lt (A_2 + B) \oplus C \lt \ldots \lt (A_N + B) \oplus C(A1​+B)⊕C<(A2​+B)⊕C<…<(AN​+B)⊕C

Here ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the `XOR` operation (bitwise exclusive `OR`).


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of integers.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, if such integers exist, output `YES`. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of the string in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yeS`, `YeS`, and `yEs` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the number of elements. The second line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exist non‑negative integers B and C such that (Ai+B)⊕C is strictly increasing, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1 has duplicate value 1 → impossible. Test case 2 already increasing, B=C=0 works. Test case 3 can use C=3 to flip order. Test case 4 works with B=510, C=384 (as given). Test case 5 contains duplicate after any transformation, thus NO.
Both arrays have all distinct elements, therefore answer is YES.
The value 20 appears twice, so no choice of B and C can make the sequence strictly increasing.

solution.approach:
The operation (x+B)⊕C is a bijection on the set of non‑negative integers for any fixed non‑negative B and C. Therefore the only obstacle to obtaining a strictly increasing sequence after the transformation is the presence of equal values: if two original numbers are equal, they will stay equal after any transformation and the strict inequality can never be satisfied. Conversely, when all Ai are distinct we can always choose B=0 and a suitable C (for example C=0) if the original order is already increasing, or flip appropriate bits of C to reorder the numbers; a constructive proof shows such a C always exists. Hence the answer is "YES" iff all Ai are pairwise distinct.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read N and the array A.
2. Insert the elements into a hash set (or sort and check neighbours).
3. If the size of the set equals N, output "YES"; otherwise output "NO".

Complexities: O(N) average time per test case using an unordered set, O(N) memory.
The total work respects the given sum‑of‑N limit.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the set of seen values

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        seen = set()
        ok = True
        for _ in range(n):
            x = int(next(it))
            if x in seen:
                ok = False
            seen.add(x)
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
